etymology: A single horizontal stroke representing the number one. The simplest of all kanji, it forms the foundation for many other characters.
Two horizontal strokes stacked — a natural extension of 一 (one).
Three horizontal strokes. The pattern of counting by stacked lines ends here — four uses a different approach.
An enclosure 囗 containing 八. Originally pictured breath being divided, suggesting separation into four parts.
The character shows a cross-hatching between two horizontal lines, representing the five elements of Chinese philosophy.
Derived from a picture of a hut or dwelling. The number six was associated with the six directions.
A cutting motion across a line — seven was considered a number of division and cutting in ancient China.
Two strokes spreading apart — representing division. Eight was the number of abundance.
A bent arm reaching for something just out of grasp — nine is one short of completion (ten).
A cross shape representing the four directions plus center, doubled — completeness and fullness.
一 (one) above 日 — originally depicted a single grain sprouting, representing abundance and the number 100.
A person 人 variant standing tall over 十 — a thousand is a great multitude of tens.
A side view of a person walking — two strokes representing legs in motion. One of the most common radicals.
A person 人 with arms stretched wide — indicating something large or great. The radical form of 'big'.
Three small marks suggesting something being divided into tiny pieces.
Three peaks representing a mountain range — the middle peak is tallest. A classic pictographic kanji.
Three flowing lines representing a river's current. Originally depicted water flowing between two banks.
A picture of the sun — a circle with a line through the middle. Used in many time-related kanji.
A crescent moon. The two inner strokes represent the surface markings visible on the moon.
A pictograph of flames rising — the two side dots represent sparks flying outward.
Flowing water depicted with a central stream and splashes on either side.
A tree with branches above and roots below — the horizontal stroke is the ground line.
Originally depicted a nugget of gold buried in the earth, with a lid or cover on top.
A cross sticking out of the ground — a mound of earth or a plant sprouting from soil.
A mark above a line — the position of something on top. A simple indicator of upward direction.
A mark below a line — the opposite of 上. Indicates downward or beneath.
A vertical line piercing through a rectangle — something in the exact center.
A hand 又 reaching toward the mouth 口 — the right hand was used for eating.
A hand 又 holding a carpenter's square 工 — the left hand assisted in crafting.
A simple picture of an open mouth. One of the most common radicals in Japanese.
A pictograph of an eye turned sideways. The inner lines represent the iris and pupil.
A drawing of the outer ear with its curves and folds.
A hand with fingers spread — the horizontal strokes represent fingers.
A kneecap 口 above a walking foot — the full leg in motion.
Rice field 田 plus strength 力 — a man was defined by his ability to work the fields.
A kneeling figure — originally a pictograph of a woman in a graceful posture.
A baby with arms outstretched — the top stroke is the head, the bottom represents swaddling.
A child 子 under a roof 冖 with teaching hands above — a place of learning.
A plant sprouting from the ground — the emergence of new life.
Legs 儿 moving forward over earth 土 — someone going ahead.
An eye 目 on legs 儿 — the act of going to look at something.
A plant pushing out of the ground, or a foot stepping out of a container. Suggests emergence.
An arrow-like shape pointing downward into an opening — entering inside.
A person 人 leaning against a tree 木 — resting in the shade.
A tree 木 with a mark at the root 一 — the root or origin of things.
Two trees 木 side by side — a small forest or grove.
Three trees 木 together — a dense forest, more than a grove 林.
A plant radical above with 化 (change) below — a plant that transforms into something beautiful.
A great 大 space above — the area above a person's head. The heavens.
A hole 穴 that has been worked 工 — an empty space, like the vast sky.
A bird's-eye view of divided rice paddies — the cross-hatching shows the irrigation paths.
A flexed arm showing muscle — raw physical strength.
Steam 气 rising from rice 米 — the invisible energy or spirit that animates life.
An enclosure 冂 with a mark inside — originally depicted a round coin, now means both circle and yen.
A person carrying grain — one full harvest cycle, representing a year.
The sun 日 with a ray of light — the color of pure sunlight. Whiteness and purity.
A cloud with rain drops falling from the sky. The top line is the sky, the box is a cloud.
Rain 雨 plus a rice field 田 with lightning — electricity from a thunderstorm.
A cart seen from above — the horizontal lines are the axle and frame, the vertical is the shaft.
Horse 馬 plus a hand — a relay station where horses were changed. Now means any station.
A cliff/building radical 广 with divination 占 inside — a place where fortune and goods are found.
A person bending over a bowl of food with a lid — the act of eating.
Food/eat radical 食 with 欠 (yawn/lack) — opening the mouth to take in liquid.
Words 言 plus tongue 舌 — speaking with the tongue, having a conversation.
Words 言 plus sell 売 — to 'trade' in words, reading and interpreting text.
A hand holding a brush 聿 creating something — the act of writing.
An ear 耳 at a gate 門 — listening at the doorway to hear what's being said.
Sun 日 plus temple 寺 — time measured by the sun's position relative to the temple.
Sunlight 日 peeking through a gate 門 — the space between the doors.
A knife 刀 dividing something in two 八 — to separate and understand.
A boat 舟 variant moving forward with a cutting edge — advancing to the front.
A foot on a road 彳 with something trailing behind — coming after.
Two people back to back — in ancient China, one turned one's back to the north.
A building facing south — in Chinese architecture, the ideal facing direction.
The sun 日 rising behind a tree 木 — the direction of sunrise, the east.
A bird settling into its nest at sunset — the direction where the sun sets.
An elder with long flowing hair — long in both physical and temporal senses.
A tall tower or pavilion — the multi-story structure reaching upward.
A woman 女 under a roof 冖 — a woman safe at home brings peace and contentment.
A tree 木 being cut with an axe 斤 — fresh wood, something newly made.
Ten 十 mouths 口 passing down stories — things told across many generations are ancient.
Hands 开 opening a gate 門 — to open up a doorway.
A crossroads — originally depicted an intersection of paths. To go, to travel.
A wheat plant with grain — something that comes in with the harvest.
A territory 囗 containing a jewel 玉 — a country's most precious treasure.
Words 言 plus 吾 (I/self) — one's own words, one's language.
A person 人 carrying something on their shoulder — 'what is that person carrying?'
Evening 夕 plus mouth 口 — calling out someone's name in the dark to identify them.
